does: This paper introduces ClawTrojan, a benchmark for evaluating vulnerabilities in LLM agents operating within local workspaces where they can read, write files, and maintain state across sessions. The core contribution is demonstrating a multi-step trojan attack paradigm where attackers embed prompt injections in files or tool outputs that agents later retrieve and execute, creating persistent control without any individual step appearing malicious in isolation. The authors show that this attack achieves 95.5% success rate against GPT-5.4 in an OpenClaw-style simulated environment, while existing defenses that inspect steps in isolation fail to detect the initial backdoor-planting write operations that enable subsequent exploitation.
connects: This work builds on emerging research in adversarial attacks against LLM agents, extending beyond single-turn prompt injection attacks that have been studied in conversational settings. It responds to the growing capability of language models as operational tools with persistent memory and tool-use abilities, which opens new attack surfaces not present in stateless chatbots. The paper connects to prior work on trojan backdoors in machine learning systems and prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLMs, but uniquely addresses the temporal and compositional dimension of attacks where multiple seemingly-benign actions compound into malicious capability across time and state persistence.
enables: This work establishes a benchmark for developing more robust defenses that track attack chains across multiple steps rather than inspecting actions in isolation. It motivates future research into defense mechanisms that can detect subtle backdoor-planting operations, validate file contents before execution, and maintain security across persistent agent state. The benchmark enables evaluation of more sophisticated defense strategies such as behavioral anomaly detection, input sanitization across time, and architectural changes to agent design that isolate untrusted content. This research direction is critical as LLM agents move from experimental systems into production deployments in corporate workspaces where such multi-step attacks pose significant real-world risks.
